summary:
The Khartoum Mesolithic, also known as the Early Khartoum culture, refers to prehistoric communities living along the Nile River near modern Khartoum in Sudan, dated to approximately 7500 to 6000 BCE. These communities are characterized by a distinctive material culture including a sophisticated bone harpoon technology, decorated wavy-line pottery that is among the earliest known in Africa, and a subsistence economy based primarily on fishing in the Nile — one of the world's richest freshwater fisheries.

The wavy-line pottery produced by Khartoum Mesolithic communities is of considerable interest because it represents an early independent tradition of ceramic production in Africa, potentially predating the adoption of pottery in the Nile Valley from Near Eastern sources. This early pottery tradition, along with related decorative styles found across the Sahara, suggests that early Holocene communities in the central Sudan and Sahara region were developing ceramic technology in ways that may have been partly independent of the pottery traditions of the Near East.

Khartoum Mesolithic communities were skilled riverine specialists who exploited the Nile's fish populations with bone harpoons of considerable technological sophistication. Their camps along the riverbanks accumulated substantial midden deposits containing fish bones, animal remains, and human burials. The culture contributes to understanding the range of economic and technological strategies available to early Holocene communities in the Nile Valley, complementing the picture provided by the early farming communities of [[event:19|the Egyptian Nile Valley]] to the north.
